brief_summary: Four-row comparison ladder between hot-swap and solder PCBs across tools required, switch changes later, plate feel, and first-build risk. Each row highlights the winning option in an amber-tinted panel — hot-swap wins three of the four rows, solder wins plate feel — matching the article's argument that hot-swap is the sane first-kit default.
data_sources: custom-keyboard-kit-buyers-guide.mdx § 'Hot-swap or solder — the one decision that's hard to undo' — 'A hot-swap PCB has small sockets under each key position that a switch plugs directly into — no soldering iron, no desoldering to change a switch later, and no risk of a cold joint or a lifted pad on a first attempt.'

custom-keyboard-kit-buyers-guide.mdx § 'Hot-swap or solder — the one decision that's hard to undo' — 'hot-swap sockets add a fractional amount of resistance and flex under the plate compared to a switch soldered directly to copper, which some experienced builders can feel as a slightly softer, less direct bottom-out.'

custom-keyboard-kit-buyers-guide.mdx § 'Hot-swap or solder — the one decision that's hard to undo' — 'Solder wins exactly one axis. Hot-swap wins the three that matter on a first build.' (InlineViz caption)
